items.passage: Humpback whales produce some of the most complex songs in the animal kingdom, lasting for many minutes. These songs travel enormous distances through ocean water because sound moves faster in liquid than in air. Males sing most often during the breeding season, and their melodies gradually change from year to year. Whales within the same region tend to share nearly identical songs. Researchers believe these shared patterns help individuals recognize members of their own population.

Green roofs are living layers of vegetation planted on top of buildings to improve urban environments. These gardens absorb rainwater that would otherwise flood streets and overwhelm drainage systems during heavy storms. The plants also cool the surrounding air through evaporation, reducing the energy needed for indoor cooling. Insulation provided by the soil keeps interior temperatures more stable across the seasons. Many cities now encourage such designs to make dense neighborhoods healthier and more pleasant.

Confirmation bias is the tendency to notice and remember information that supports what a person already believes. When people search for evidence, they often favor sources that agree with their existing opinions. Facts that contradict those opinions are frequently ignored, dismissed, or quickly forgotten. This habit can make individuals feel more certain than the available evidence truly justifies. Being aware of the pattern helps people weigh opposing arguments more fairly before reaching a conclusion.

The spice trade shaped the economies of Europe and Asia for many centuries before modern times. Merchants carried pepper, cinnamon, and cloves along dangerous routes that stretched across oceans and vast deserts. Because these goods were rare and highly valued, they often sold for enormous profits in distant markets. European powers competed fiercely to control the ports where such cargo changed hands. This rivalry encouraged long voyages of exploration that eventually connected continents through permanent trading networks.

Volcanic mountains form when molten rock rises from deep inside the earth and erupts onto the surface. Each eruption adds fresh layers of lava and ash that gradually build the slopes higher over time. Some of these peaks grow along the boundaries where two great plates slowly collide or pull apart. Others appear above isolated hot spots that remain fixed while the crust drifts above them. Over thousands of years, repeated activity can create towering cones visible for miles.

Robotic rovers have explored the surface of Mars for decades, sending valuable data back to Earth. These machines travel slowly across rocky terrain, carefully avoiding steep slopes and sharp boulders along the way. Cameras and instruments mounted on them examine soil, rocks, and the thin atmosphere for signs of ancient water. Because commands take many minutes to arrive, each rover must handle unexpected obstacles on its own. Discoveries from these missions continue to reshape how scientists understand the red planet.
